## 3. Root Cause (with evidence chain)

**Category:** configuration / change management (network egress change). **Cynefin domain:** Complicated — cause and effect were fully discoverable through analysis; the data existed internally from day one.

**Statement:** On 2026-04-28 at 13:50 UTC, infra change `infra-2214` (egress-NAT cutover, part of a VPC move, undertaken for ~$3.1k/mo NAT-gateway savings [30]) changed the source IP addresses of Trellis's webhook-dispatcher traffic. Public documentation [28] told firewall-restricted customers to allowlist 34.96.18.4–34.96.18.7 and promised those addresses were "stable" with "90 days advance notice" of changes. No notice was given. Every customer enforcing that allowlist began dropping Trellis's webhook traffic at the firewall (manifesting as ETIMEDOUT, not an HTTP error), starting within 32 minutes of the cutover [3].

**Why the evidence is conclusive, not circumstantial:**

1. **Temporal precision.** Same endpoints: 200 OK throughout 4/27; ETIMEDOUT starting 14:22 on 4/28, 32 minutes after the deploy marker in the same log [3].
2. **Mechanism confirmed by a customer.** Ostrander's IT observed inbound attempts from IPs *not on the documented allowlist* [11] — direct observation of the new egress IPs hitting their firewall. This matches sre.dmitri's own (unanswered) hypothesis from 4/30 [12].
3. **Victim selection matches the mechanism.** Only firewall-allowlisting customers were hit ("mostly the same dozen endpoints" [12]); the broad customer base was fine, which is exactly why the status page stayed green and the send queue kept draining [23]. A pricing or outage cause cannot produce this victim pattern.
4. **Independent corroboration per account.** All four churned accounts have webhook-failure evidence dated to 4/28: Hartwell [3][7][21][38], Ostrander [3][11], Vela [3][4][33], Pinnacle [15][21].
5. **The failure mode (ETIMEDOUT vs. HTTP 4xx/5xx)** is the signature of packets dropped by a firewall, not an unhealthy customer endpoint — consistent with customers reporting "our endpoint is healthy, nothing changed on our side" [7].

**Five Whys (to the systemic layer):**
1. Why did customers churn? → Their event feeds silently died for weeks while support insisted nothing was wrong.
2. Why did the feeds die? → Customer firewalls dropped traffic from unrecognized source IPs.
3. Why were the IPs unrecognized? → infra-2214 changed egress IPs without honoring the documented 90-day notice commitment [28].
4. Why did the change ship without notice? → The change process had no step linking network egress changes to externally published commitments (docs last updated 2024-11; no doc/comms review in the cutover).
5. Why did it stay broken for 6+ weeks? → No SLI measured *delivery success from the customer's perspective*; the alerting design actively suppressed the signal [21]; and no escalation path existed for a cluster of similar timeout alerts ("parking it for now," no replies [12]).

**Swiss-cheese alignment (which layers had holes):**

| Layer | Hole |
|---|---|
| Change management | Egress-IP change shipped with no customer notice, violating a published 90-day commitment [28][30] |
| Monitoring/alerting | Delivery-timeout alerts existed but were dismissable; after 5/5 retries the system *parked deliveries and suppressed repeat alerts* — designed silence [21] |
| Engineering triage | Correct hypothesis raised 4/30, then parked with zero follow-up [12]; retro later certified "no incidents attributed" [30] |
| Support | Pattern of identical tickets closed as customer-side because "status page is green" [7][23]; no mechanism to correlate ticket clusters with recent deploys |
| Customer success | 90% consumption drop flagged but rationalized as "seasonal lull" [38]; Priya's direct question went unanswered [33] |

No single layer caused the churn; the alignment of all five did. Blameless framing applies: every individual acted reasonably given the information their tools surfaced. The tools surfaced the wrong picture.

**Status:** there is **no evidence in the dump that the issue has been fixed or disclosed**. Treat the failure as ongoing for any allowlisting customer who has not manually updated firewall rules (high confidence that at least the parked endpoints — including soon-to-churn accounts — are still parked as of 5/18 [21]).

---

## 4. Account-by-Account Attribution

| Account | Panel / MRR | Outcome | Cause | Evidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Hartwell Group | Scale, $2,400/mo | Churn eff. 6/30 [16] | Webhook/egress-IP failure | ETIMEDOUT 4/28 [3]; consumption −90% from 4/28 [38]; ticket #4821 dismissed by support [7]; deliveries parked 5/18 [21]; **price-exempt** [8] |
| Ostrander Logistics | Growth, $1,850/mo | Churn eff. 6/15 [16] | Webhook/egress-IP failure | ETIMEDOUT 4/28 [3]; their IT identified non-allowlisted source IPs, escalation unresolved [11][28]; **price-exempt** [8] |
| Vela Robotics | Scale, $3,100/mo | Notice 5/22 [16] | Webhook/egress-IP failure | ETIMEDOUT 4/28 [3]; NPS 1 "integrations silently died for three weeks" [4]; declined QBR pending an explanation that never came [33]; **price-exempt** [8] |
| Pinnacle Claims | Growth, $1,650/mo | Non-renewal, term ends 6/30 [16] | Webhook/egress-IP failure | "Dead since end of April," four unanswered asks [15]; first failure 4/28, parked 5/18 [21]; **price-exempt** [8] |
| Meridian Health | $2,000/mo | Downgrade to $1,200/mo eff. July [20] | May 12 outage (+budget) | NPS 3 citing outage [19]; retained, not churn [20] |

All four churned logos: one cause, one start date, zero exposure to the price increase.

---

## 5. Financial Impact (arithmetic shown)

**Churned MRR (webhook root cause):**

```
Hartwell   2,400
Ostrander  1,850
Vela       3,100
Pinnacle   1,650
─────────────────
Total      9,000  /mo
× 12 = 108,000   /yr annualized
```

**Outage-attributable contraction:** Meridian downgrade = 2,000 − 1,200 = **$800/mo** [20] (plus one-time SLA credits to 40 enterprise accounts [14], amount not in evidence — *not estimated*).

**Pricing-attributable churn: $0.** No lost account paid the new price [8]; the loudest pricing objectors expanded [18].

**Total monthly run-rate impact: 9,000 + 800 = $9,800/mo.**

**Context worth stating to the CFO:** the cutover saves ~$3.1k/mo in NAT costs [30]; the way it shipped destroyed $9.0k/mo in MRR — a ~3:1 destruction-to-savings ratio before counting renewal-season contagion risk, support load, and reputation cost with the remaining "dozen endpoints" [12] cohort.

**Assumptions, flagged:** (a) CRM-panel MRR figures are current at churn date — high confidence; (b) no other allowlisting customers have churned yet but remain at risk — medium confidence (the dump shows ~a dozen affected endpoints [12] and only four churns plus Hartwell/Pinnacle parked [21]; the others are unaccounted for); (c) SLA credit cost excluded for lack of data.

---

## 6. Honest Assessment of the Competing Theories

### Theory 1 — "It's the April price increase" (Marta [1], echoed by CEO [26])
**Verdict: contradicted by the evidence on every testable point.**
- All four lost logos were in the legacy-2024 cohort whose "rates don't change" [8]. They had no price increase to react to.
- The accounts that *did* complain about price behaved oppositely: Brightside escalated angrily [2] then **signed an annual renewal at the new price** [18]; Kelco threatened to evaluate alternatives [13] then **added 5 seats** [18] with usage up 22% MoM.
- The "they went dark right after it landed" observation [1] is a real correlation with the wrong cause: the accounts went dark right after **the 4/28 egress cutover**, which happened to land weeks after the pricing announcement. The social-media noise [24] measured sentiment, not churn.
- **Note the process failure in [35]:** "send me anything that contradicts that by EOD Thursday or it ships as-is" is burden-of-proof inversion on a board-level claim. This document is that contradiction. Rolling back pricing [1] would sacrifice revenue to fix a problem pricing didn't cause — and leave the actual cause running into renewal season.

### Theory 2 — "It's the May 12 outage"
**Verdict: real incident, wrong churn driver; second-order revenue impact only.**
- The outage (3h02m = 182 min, verified against the 09:14–12:16 window [6][36]) postdates the churn-driving failures by two weeks: all four accounts' webhook failures began 4/28 [3][21], and two had already escalated before the outage — Hartwell to its CTO on 5/6 [7], Ostrander's unresolved engineering escalation on 5/11–12 [11]. (Vela's and Pinnacle's documented dissatisfaction is dated 5/19–5/26 [33][4][15], but cites failures running since end of April.)
- Outage-affected accounts that received the credit-and-comms treatment stayed: Tatum & Co renewed 12 months [9][27]. The contrast is the strongest internal control in the dataset: **acknowledged failure + honest comms → retention; denied failure + boilerplate → churn.** The outage cost $800/mo (Meridian) [20]; the denial pattern cost $9,000/mo.

### Theory 3 — "Customer-side firewall/config problems" (support's working theory [7][23], dmitri's parking rationale [12])
**Verdict: half-right about the mechanism, wrong about the responsibility.** The drops did happen at customer firewalls — because Trellis changed the IPs it had documented as stable [28]. Closing those tickets as customer-side converted a fixable engineering issue into a trust collapse ("Asked and answered. It is your side." [7]).

### Noise register (examined and excluded)
Items with no bearing on the churn question: spam-folder ticket [5], feature request [22], invoice PO reissue [31], CSV/UTF-8 bug [17][34], customer-side SSO cert [29][32], push-notification delay [25], annual-billing inquiry [37], positive NPS [10]. The Calloway and Tatum items additionally serve as controls: both are in the same price-exempt cohort [8], neither shows any webhook-failure evidence in the dump (the dump says nothing about their firewall configurations — *inference from absence of complaints, medium confidence*), and both retained — isolating the webhook variable.

---

## 7. What Monitoring Would Have Caught This Before Customers Did

Detection actually went: the first customer report arrived within six days (Hartwell's ticket, 5/4 [7]); Trellis's own CS usage model had even surfaced the signal on 4/29 — Hartwell's health score dropped 82→61 overnight on a ~90% consumption collapse [38] — but it was rationalized as a "seasonal lull." Trellis never internally diagnosed the cause at all; Ostrander handed over the diagnosis on 5/11 [11] and it still sat unresolved. Time-to-diagnose: **never (internal)**. The fixes, in order of leverage:

1. **Webhook delivery-success SLI, measured from the customer's perspective.** SLI: % of webhook deliveries acknowledged 2xx within SLA, per endpoint and in aggregate. SLO example: ≥99.5% rolling-7d per active endpoint. The 4/28 event was a step-function to ~0% for a dozen endpoints — a fast-burn page within minutes. ("Send queue is draining fine" [23] measured attempts, not deliveries; that is monitoring theater.)
2. **Never suppress alerts on parked/dead-lettered deliveries — invert them.** "5/5 retries exhausted, parking deliveries (suppressing repeat alerts)" [21] is the system deciding a sustained total failure is too noisy to mention. Parking an endpoint should page once, open a ticket automatically, and notify the customer ("we are unable to deliver to your endpoint since <date>"). A customer-facing per-endpoint delivery dashboard removes the status-page blind spot entirely.
3. **Deploy-correlated anomaly detection.** Any cluster of new delivery timeouts within N hours of a deploy marker (the marker was *in the same log file* [3]) should auto-link to the change and block the change from being retro'd as "no incidents attributed" [30] until the alert cluster is dispositioned. The 4/30 "parking it for now" message [12] needed an owner and a deadline, not a shrug — heuristic: an alert cluster touching a dozen paying customers' endpoints is never "parked."
4. **Egress-IP change control as a product commitment, not an infra detail.** The docs promise 90 days' notice [28]. Encode that: any change to egress IPs requires (a) docs update, (b) customer notification campaign to allowlisting customers, (c) dual-running old and new IPs through the notice window. Pre-flight check in the cutover runbook: "does this change any externally documented address?"
5. **Usage-drop alerts wired to CS playbooks.** A 90% day-over-day consumption drop [38] should trigger a same-week outreach with an engineering correlation check, not a "could be seasonal" note for the next sync. Threshold suggestion: >50% sustained 48h drop on any account ≥$1k MRR.
6. **Support ticket clustering.** Two-plus tickets matching "stopped receiving events" in a week [23] should auto-escalate to engineering with deploy-timeline context, and support agents should lose the ability to close such tickets as customer-side while a correlated alert cluster is open.

Items 1–2 alone would have produced a page at ~14:30 UTC on 4/28 — six days before the first customer ticket and 45 days (~6.5 weeks) before this report.

---

## 8. Immediate Actions (before renewal season)

| # | Action | Owner (role) | Due | Priority |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Confirm current egress IPs; restore documented IPs or publish new ones; update docs page [28]; notify all allowlisting customers (start with the "dozen endpoints" cohort [12] and parked endpoints [21]) | Platform Eng lead | 48h | P1 |
| 2 | Replay/park-release queued deliveries where endpoints recover; tell each affected customer exactly what happened and when (the Tatum playbook [9][27] is the proven template) | Platform Eng + CS | 1 wk | P1 |
| 3 | Correct the Friday exec narrative: present churn cut by cause per CFO's request [26] using §4–§6; do **not** ship "pricing-driven" [35]; pause any pricing rollback decision [1] | Ops lead (you) + CFO | Before Friday review | P1 |
| 4 | Last-ditch saves: Hartwell (eff. 6/30) and Pinnacle (6/30) have not lapsed yet — executive apology + root-cause letter + credits + fix confirmation | CS lead + exec sponsor | 72h | P1 |
| 5 | Resolve Ostrander escalation #4903 [11] with a written root-cause answer (also owed to Vela's champion [33]) even if churn stands — protects references and re-entry | CS lead | 1 wk | P2 |
| 6 | Ship monitoring items §7.1–.3; re-open the infra-2214 retro [30] and run a blameless postmortem covering the full 4/28→6/12 arc, including the support and alert-suppression layers | Eng manager | 2 wks | P2 |
| 7 | Audit remaining allowlisting customers (docs-page referrers, firewall-related tickets, the unparked remainder of the dozen endpoints) for silent failure | Support ops | 1 wk | P1 |

**Lessons (for the postmortem):** (1) A green status page plus a draining send queue measures Trellis's ability to *send*, not customers' ability to *receive* — the only SLI that matters is the customer-perceived one. (2) Alert suppression converted a P1 into silence by design; suppression policies need a "sustained total failure" carve-out. (3) Where we got lucky: Ostrander's IT literally handed over the diagnosis [11], Tatum/Calloway act as natural controls, and two of four churns haven't lapsed yet — there is still a save window.

---

*Precision check: all totals verified by explicit arithmetic (§5); all dates cross-checked against day-of-week and inter-item consistency (4/28 = Tuesday [3][7][12]; 22 days ≈ Vela's "three weeks" [4]); every causal claim carries at least two independent evidence items; assumptions and unknowns (SLA credit cost, current fix status, remaining at-risk endpoints) are explicitly flagged with confidence levels.*

**To:** Devon (Head of Platform)
**Cc:** Renata Iwu (VP Product), Billing Ops, Jonah (PM), #atlas-migration
